subject: Nephrology
question: A 70-year-old man with CHF on furosemide and lisinopril presents with oliguria and creatinine rise from 1.0 to 2.8 mg/dL over 3 days. BUN/Cr ratio 28, urine Na <20, FENa <1%. What type of AKI?

options.text: Pre-renal AKI due to volume depletion + ACEi
options.explanation: Pre-renal AKI: BUN/Cr >20, UNa <20, FENa <1%. ACEi impairs renal autoregulation. Furosemide causes volume depletion. Hold nephrotoxic agents, IV fluids, monitor output.
ATN shows muddy brown casts, FENa >2%, UNa >40. Caused by prolonged ischemia or nephrotoxins. Recovery takes weeks.
AIN shows WBC casts, eosinophiluria, fever, rash. Caused by drugs (NSAIDs, antibiotics, PPIs). FENa >1% unlike pre-renal.
Post-renal shows hydronephrosis on ultrasound. Caused by BPH, stones, tumors. Normal BUN/Cr, variable UNa.
GN shows hematuria, RBC casts, proteinuria, hypertension. FENa <1% but active urinary sediment differentiates from pre-renal.

educationalObjective: Differentiate types of AKI using BUN/Cr ratio, urine Na, FENa, and urinary sediment.
keyTakeaway: Pre-renal: BUN/Cr >20, UNa <20, FENa <1%. ATN: FENa >2%, muddy brown casts. AIN: WBC casts, eosinophiluria.
